biomarkers.requirement: Broad molecular tumor profiling recommended to identify rare driver alterations

trial_data.description: First-line nivolumab plus ipilimumab vs chemotherapy in patients with unresectable malignant pleural mesothelioma. Referenced with 3-year outcomes. Data extrapolated from pleural mesothelioma to peritoneal mesothelioma.

tier_1_regimens.notes: This regimen is a preferred first-line option for patients with epithelioid peritoneal mesothelioma who are medically inoperable, have complete cytoreduction not achievable, or refuse surgery. The CheckMate 743 trial demonstrated efficacy in pleural mesothelioma, and the NCCN Panel extrapolates these results to peritoneal mesothelioma [ref 107]. Note footnote d: Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab; pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph has different dosing and administration instructions compared to IV pembrolizumab. Note footnote f: Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy is NOT approved for concurrent use with IV ipilimumab; however, for nivolumab monotherapy, nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV nivolumab. All regimens may also be used for pericardial mesothelioma and tunica vaginalis testis mesothelioma [ref a]. Broad molecular tumor profiling is recommended with the goal of identifying rare driver alterations (eg, NTRK or ALK) for which effective drugs may be available or to appropriately counsel patients regarding availability of clinical trials [refs 27,28]. This regimen is also listed as Preferred for subsequent therapy after chemotherapy first-line. PS 0-2 required.

trial_data.description: Pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy vs chemotherapy in untreated advanced pleural mesothelioma. Phase 3, open-label, randomised controlled trial. Referenced from Lancet 2023;402:2295-2306. Data extrapolated from pleural to peritoneal mesothelioma.

tier_1_regimens.notes: This triplet regimen is a preferred first-line option for patients with epithelioid peritoneal mesothelioma who are medically inoperable, cannot achieve complete cytoreduction, or refuse surgery. Carboplatin may be substituted if patients are not candidates for cisplatin. The trial was conducted in pleural mesothelioma and data are extrapolated to peritoneal mesothelioma per the NCCN Panel [ref 10]. Note footnote d: Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab; the SC formulation has different dosing and administration instructions compared to IV pembrolizumab. All regimens may also be used for pericardial mesothelioma and tunica vaginalis testis mesothelioma [ref a].

trial_data.description: Bevacizumab for newly diagnosed pleural mesothelioma in a randomised, controlled, open-label, phase 3 trial. Referenced from Lancet 2016;387:1405-1414. Data extrapolated from pleural to peritoneal mesothelioma.

tier_1_regimens.notes: This triplet is a preferred first-line option for epithelioid peritoneal mesothelioma in medically inoperable patients or those who refuse surgery. Carboplatin may be used if patients are not candidates for cisplatin. An FDA-approved biosimilar is an appropriate substitute for bevacizumab [ref c]. If a bevacizumab-containing regimen is administered, there should be at least a 6-week interval between the last dose and cytoreductive surgery (CRS) [refs 7,8]. The MAPS trial established the benefit of adding bevacizumab to cisplatin/pemetrexed in pleural mesothelioma; the NCCN Panel extrapolates this to peritoneal mesothelioma [ref 106]. This regimen is also listed as Preferred for subsequent therapy after immunotherapy first-line, and as Other Recommended for subsequent therapy in biphasic/sarcomatoid histology first-line. All regimens may also be used for pericardial mesothelioma and tunica vaginalis testis mesothelioma [ref a].

trial_data.trial_name: Multiple trials (Vogelzang et al. 2003; Janne et al. 2005; Carteni et al. 2009; Castagneto et al. 2008; Ceresoli et al. 2006; Santoro et al. 2008)
trial_data.description: Multiple clinical trials including a phase III study of pemetrexed with cisplatin vs cisplatin alone in malignant pleural mesothelioma [ref 1], expanded access programs in peritoneal mesothelioma [refs 2,3], and phase II studies of pemetrexed plus carboplatin [refs 4,5]. EAP data for 109 patients with peritoneal mesothelioma: for pemetrexed plus cisplatin, 1-year survival was 57.4% (95% CI, 10.3%-100%). For pemetrexed alone, median survival was 10.3 months; 1-year survival was 41.5% (95% CI, 4.6%-78.4%).

tier_1_regimens.notes: This doublet is a preferred first-line option for epithelioid peritoneal mesothelioma in medically inoperable patients or those who refuse surgery. Carboplatin may be used if patients are not candidates for cisplatin. The combination of pemetrexed with platinum has been studied in multiple trials including the pivotal phase III trial in pleural mesothelioma [ref 1], with extrapolation to peritoneal mesothelioma. The International Expanded Access Program included 109 patients with peritoneal mesothelioma; for pemetrexed plus cisplatin, 1-year survival was 57.4% [refs 7,92-94]. Japanese studies in peritoneal mesothelioma reported median OS of 15.4-16.6 months with pemetrexed plus cisplatin [refs 87,89,95]. This regimen is also listed as Preferred for subsequent therapy after immunotherapy first-line and as Other Recommended for subsequent therapy. Carboplatin is recommended if patients are not candidates for cisplatin, regardless of histology [refs 7,92]. All regimens may also be used for pericardial mesothelioma and tunica vaginalis testis mesothelioma [ref a].

trial_data.trial_name: Multiple phase II studies (Nowak et al. 2002; van Haarst et al. 2002; Favaretto et al. 2003)
trial_data.description: Multicentre phase II studies of cisplatin and gemcitabine for malignant mesothelioma [refs 12,13], and gemcitabine combined with carboplatin [ref 14]. Data from pleural mesothelioma extrapolated to peritoneal mesothelioma.

tier_1_regimens.notes: This doublet is listed as Useful in Certain Circumstances for first-line treatment of epithelioid peritoneal mesothelioma in medically inoperable patients or those who refuse surgery. Several small studies in patients with peritoneal mesothelioma have reported responses to first-line therapy with gemcitabine plus cisplatin [refs 96-98]. Data also show that first-line therapy with gemcitabine plus pemetrexed is effective, although this regimen is toxic (grade 3-4 neutropenia, 60%) [ref 99]. Carboplatin may be used if patients are not candidates for cisplatin. All regimens may also be used for pericardial mesothelioma and tunica vaginalis testis mesothelioma [ref a].

trial_data.trial_name: International Expanded Access Program (Taylor et al. 2008)
trial_data.description: Single-agent pemetrexed for chemonaive and pretreated patients with malignant pleural mesothelioma. Data extrapolated to peritoneal mesothelioma.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Single-agent pemetrexed is listed as Useful in Certain Circumstances for first-line treatment of epithelioid peritoneal mesothelioma in medically inoperable patients or those who refuse surgery. From the EAP data for peritoneal mesothelioma, pemetrexed alone yielded a median survival of 10.3 months and 1-year survival of 41.5% (95% CI, 4.6%-78.4%) [refs 7,92-94]. All regimens may also be used for pericardial mesothelioma and tunica vaginalis testis mesothelioma [ref a].

trial_data.description: Active symptom control with or without chemotherapy in the treatment of patients with malignant pleural mesothelioma. A multicentre randomised trial. Data extrapolated to peritoneal mesothelioma.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Vinorelbine is listed as Useful in Certain Circumstances for first-line treatment of epithelioid peritoneal mesothelioma in medically inoperable patients or those who refuse surgery. Evidence is primarily from pleural mesothelioma trials extrapolated to peritoneal mesothelioma. All regimens may also be used for pericardial mesothelioma and tunica vaginalis testis mesothelioma [ref a].

biomarkers.requirement: Broad molecular tumor profiling recommended to identify rare driver alterations

trial_data.description: First-line nivolumab plus ipilimumab vs chemotherapy in patients with unresectable malignant pleural mesothelioma. 3-year outcomes from Ann Oncol 2022;33:488-499. This is the PREFERRED regimen specifically for biphasic/sarcomatoid histology based on CheckMate 743 data showing greater benefit in non-epithelioid subtypes.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Ipilimumab plus nivolumab is the PREFERRED first-line option for patients with biphasic or sarcomatoid peritoneal mesothelioma who are medically inoperable, cannot achieve complete cytoreduction, or refuse surgery. This is the top-preferred regimen for this histology based on the CheckMate 743 trial, which showed particular benefit in non-epithelioid (biphasic/sarcomatoid) subtypes [refs 107,11]. The NCCN Panel extrapolates these pleural mesothelioma data to peritoneal mesothelioma. Note footnote f: Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy is NOT approved for concurrent use with IV ipilimumab; however, for nivolumab monotherapy, nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy SC may be substituted for IV nivolumab. PS 0-2 required. All regimens may also be used for pericardial mesothelioma and tunica vaginalis testis mesothelioma [ref a].

trial_data.description: Pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy vs chemotherapy in untreated advanced pleural mesothelioma. Referenced from Lancet 2023;402:2295-2306. Data extrapolated from pleural to peritoneal mesothelioma.

tier_1_regimens.notes: This triplet regimen is a preferred first-line option for biphasic or sarcomatoid peritoneal mesothelioma in medically inoperable patients or those who refuse surgery. It is listed alongside ipilimumab plus nivolumab as a preferred regimen for this histology. Carboplatin may be substituted if patients are not candidates for cisplatin. Note footnote d: Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab; the SC formulation has different dosing and administration instructions. All regimens may also be used for pericardial mesothelioma and tunica vaginalis testis mesothelioma [ref a].

trial_data.description: Bevacizumab for newly diagnosed pleural mesothelioma. Lancet 2016;387:1405-1414. Data extrapolated from pleural to peritoneal mesothelioma.

tier_1_regimens.notes: For biphasic or sarcomatoid peritoneal mesothelioma, this triplet is classified as Other Recommended (rather than Preferred) for first-line therapy in medically inoperable patients or those who refuse surgery. This contrasts with epithelioid histology where it is Preferred. An FDA-approved biosimilar is an appropriate substitute for bevacizumab [ref c]. If a bevacizumab-containing regimen is administered, there should be at least a 6-week interval between the last dose and CRS [refs 7,8]. The MAPS trial established the benefit of bevacizumab in pleural mesothelioma [ref 106]. All regimens may also be used for pericardial mesothelioma and tunica vaginalis testis mesothelioma [ref a].

trial_data.trial_name: Multiple trials (Vogelzang et al. 2003; Janne et al. 2005; Carteni et al. 2009; Castagneto et al. 2008; Ceresoli et al. 2006; Santoro et al. 2008)
trial_data.description: Multiple clinical trials in pleural and peritoneal mesothelioma as described in epithelioid first-line entry. EAP data for peritoneal mesothelioma: pemetrexed plus cisplatin 1-year survival 57.4%.

tier_1_regimens.notes: For biphasic or sarcomatoid peritoneal mesothelioma, this doublet is classified as Other Recommended for first-line therapy. This is a lower preference level compared to epithelioid histology where it is Preferred. Carboplatin may be used if patients are not candidates for cisplatin, regardless of histology [refs 7,92]. The evidence base is from pleural mesothelioma trials extrapolated to peritoneal mesothelioma. All regimens may also be used for pericardial mesothelioma and tunica vaginalis testis mesothelioma [ref a].

trial_data.trial_name: Multiple phase II studies (Nowak et al. 2002; van Haarst et al. 2002; Favaretto et al. 2003)
trial_data.description: Same trials as epithelioid first-line entry. Data extrapolated from pleural to peritoneal mesothelioma.

tier_1_regimens.notes: For biphasic or sarcomatoid peritoneal mesothelioma, this doublet is listed as Useful in Certain Circumstances for first-line therapy. Carboplatin may be used if patients are not candidates for cisplatin. Small studies in peritoneal mesothelioma have reported responses to gemcitabine plus cisplatin [refs 96-98]. All regimens may also be used for pericardial mesothelioma and tunica vaginalis testis mesothelioma [ref a].

trial_data.trial_name: International Expanded Access Program (Taylor et al. 2008)
trial_data.description: Single-agent pemetrexed for chemonaive and pretreated patients with malignant pleural mesothelioma. Data extrapolated to peritoneal mesothelioma.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Single-agent pemetrexed is listed as Useful in Certain Circumstances for first-line treatment of biphasic or sarcomatoid peritoneal mesothelioma. Evidence is from pleural mesothelioma trials extrapolated to peritoneal mesothelioma. All regimens may also be used for pericardial mesothelioma and tunica vaginalis testis mesothelioma [ref a].

trial_data.description: Active symptom control with or without chemotherapy in malignant pleural mesothelioma. Multicentre randomised trial. Data extrapolated to peritoneal mesothelioma.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Vinorelbine is listed as Useful in Certain Circumstances for first-line treatment of biphasic or sarcomatoid peritoneal mesothelioma. Evidence is from pleural mesothelioma trials. All regimens may also be used for pericardial mesothelioma and tunica vaginalis testis mesothelioma [ref a].

trial_data.description: Nivolumab vs placebo in patients with relapsed malignant mesothelioma. Lancet Oncol 2021;22:1530-1540. Most patients had pleural mesothelioma (95%) and epithelioid histology (88%); a few patients had peritoneal mesothelioma (n=16). Median overall survival was 10.2 months (95% CI, 8.5-12.1) for nivolumab vs 6.9 months (95% CI, 5.0-8.0) for placebo (HR, 0.69; 95% CI, 0.52-0.91). Grade 3 or worse adverse events were 3% for nivolumab.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Nivolumab monotherapy is a preferred subsequent therapy option for patients with peritoneal mesothelioma (any histology) whose disease has progressed after first-line platinum-based chemotherapy. This is based on the CONFIRM phase 3 trial which demonstrated improved OS (10.2 vs 6.9 months, HR 0.69) in relapsed mesothelioma [ref 88]. Note footnote f: Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV nivolumab for monotherapy; the SC formulation has different dosing and administration instructions. Immune checkpoint inhibitors are associated with unique immune-mediated adverse events such as endocrine disorders; providers should be aware of these toxicities and manage them appropriately [see NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immunotherapy-Related Toxicities]. Consider rechallenge with pemetrexed if good response to front-line pemetrexed-based treatment [ref e,29]. All regimens may also be used for pericardial mesothelioma and tunica vaginalis testis mesothelioma [ref a].

trial_data.description: MAPS2: Nivolumab or nivolumab plus ipilimumab in patients with relapsed malignant pleural mesothelioma. Multicentre, open-label, randomised, non-comparative, phase 2 trial. Lancet Oncol 2019;20:239-253. INITIATE: Ipilimumab and nivolumab in the treatment of recurrent malignant pleural mesothelioma. Prospective, single-arm, phase 2 trial. Lancet Respir Med 2019;7:260-270. Data extrapolated from pleural to peritoneal mesothelioma.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Ipilimumab plus nivolumab is a preferred subsequent therapy option for patients with peritoneal mesothelioma (any histology) whose disease has progressed after first-line platinum-based chemotherapy. Evidence is from the MAPS2 and INITIATE phase 2 trials in relapsed pleural mesothelioma, extrapolated to peritoneal mesothelioma [refs 113,19]. A cohort study in 29 patients with peritoneal mesothelioma receiving immune checkpoint inhibitors (69% received nivolumab plus ipilimumab) showed overall response rate of 19% (95% CI, 6.6%-39%) and median OS of 19 months (95% CI, 7.4-43) [ref 100]. Note footnote f: Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy is NOT approved for concurrent use with IV ipilimumab. Consider rechallenge if good response to front-line treatment [ref e,29]. All regimens may also be used for pericardial mesothelioma and tunica vaginalis testis mesothelioma [ref a].

trial_data.description: Referenced from multiple trials as described in first-line entries. Consider rechallenge if good response to front-line pemetrexed-based treatment.

tier_1_regimens.notes: This doublet is a preferred subsequent therapy option for patients with peritoneal mesothelioma (any histology) whose disease has progressed after first-line ipilimumab plus nivolumab. Consider rechallenge if good sustained response was obtained when the initial pemetrexed-based chemotherapy was interrupted [ref e,29]. Carboplatin may be used if patients are not candidates for cisplatin. All regimens may also be used for pericardial mesothelioma and tunica vaginalis testis mesothelioma [ref a].

trial_data.description: Bevacizumab for newly diagnosed pleural mesothelioma. Lancet 2016;387:1405-1414.

tier_1_regimens.notes: This triplet is a preferred subsequent therapy option for patients with peritoneal mesothelioma (any histology) whose disease has progressed after first-line ipilimumab plus nivolumab. An FDA-approved biosimilar is an appropriate substitute for bevacizumab [ref c]. If a bevacizumab-containing regimen is administered, there should be at least a 6-week interval between the last dose and CRS [refs 7,8]. Consider rechallenge if good sustained response to front-line treatment [ref e,29]. All regimens may also be used for pericardial mesothelioma and tunica vaginalis testis mesothelioma [ref a].

trial_data.trial_name: Phase III trial (Jassem et al. 2008) and retrospective survey (Zucali et al. 2012)
trial_data.description: Phase III trial of pemetrexed plus best supportive care compared with best supportive care in previously treated patients with advanced malignant pleural mesothelioma [ref 20]. Second-line chemotherapy in malignant pleural mesothelioma: retrospective multicenter survey [ref 21].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Single-agent pemetrexed is classified as Other Recommended for subsequent therapy in peritoneal mesothelioma (any histology). The phase III trial demonstrated benefit of pemetrexed plus best supportive care over best supportive care alone in previously treated pleural mesothelioma [ref 20]. Consider rechallenge if good sustained response to front-line pemetrexed-based treatment [ref e,29]. All regimens may also be used for pericardial mesothelioma and tunica vaginalis testis mesothelioma [ref a].

trial_data.description: Efficacy, safety, and biomarker analysis of combined PD-L1 (atezolizumab) and VEGF (bevacizumab) blockade in advanced malignant peritoneal mesothelioma. Cancer Discov 2021;11:2738-2747. Phase 2 trial in 20 patients with advanced and unresectable peritoneal mesothelioma who had progressed on or were intolerant to pemetrexed plus platinum chemotherapy. Many patients were women (60%) and did not have previous exposure to asbestos (75%). Median age was 63 years. Most had epithelioid histology (90%); 10% had biphasic histology. Response rate was 40% (8/20; 95% CI, 19%-64%). Overall survival at 1 year was 85% (95% CI, 60%-95%). Grade 3 treatment-emergent adverse events in 50% including hypertension (40%) and anemia (10%).

tier_1_regimens.notes: Atezolizumab plus bevacizumab is classified as Other Recommended for subsequent therapy in peritoneal mesothelioma. IMPORTANT: This regimen should only be considered if patients have NOT been previously treated with immune checkpoint inhibitors [ref h]. This is the only trial specifically conducted in peritoneal mesothelioma (not pleural mesothelioma) for this regimen, with an ORR of 40% and 1-year OS of 85% [ref 85]. Note footnote g: Atezolizumab and hyaluronidase-tqjs subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV atezolizumab; the SC formulation has different dosing and administration instructions. An FDA-approved biosimilar is an appropriate substitute for bevacizumab [ref c]. All regimens may also be used for pericardial mesothelioma and tunica vaginalis testis mesothelioma [ref a].

trial_data.trial_name: Manegold et al. 2005; van Meerbeeck et al. 1999
trial_data.description: Second-line chemotherapy received by patients treated in the phase III trial of pemetrexed plus cisplatin [ref 23]. Phase II study of gemcitabine in patients with malignant pleural mesothelioma [ref 24].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Single-agent gemcitabine is classified as Other Recommended for subsequent therapy in peritoneal mesothelioma (any histology). Evidence is from second-line studies in pleural mesothelioma extrapolated to peritoneal mesothelioma. In a Japanese retrospective study, second-line gemcitabine was used in 12 of 26 patients with peritoneal mesothelioma [ref 87]. All regimens may also be used for pericardial mesothelioma and tunica vaginalis testis mesothelioma [ref a].

trial_data.trial_name: Stebbing et al. 2009; Zauderer et al. 2014
trial_data.description: Efficacy and safety of weekly vinorelbine in relapsed malignant pleural mesothelioma [ref 25]. Vinorelbine and gemcitabine as second- or third-line therapy for malignant pleural mesothelioma [ref 26].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Vinorelbine is classified as Other Recommended for subsequent therapy in peritoneal mesothelioma (any histology). Evidence is from pleural mesothelioma studies extrapolated to peritoneal mesothelioma. All regimens may also be used for pericardial mesothelioma and tunica vaginalis testis mesothelioma [ref a].

trial_data.description: The Chicago Consensus on peritoneal surface malignancies: Management of peritoneal mesothelioma. Cancer 2020;126:2547-2552.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Cisplatin plus doxorubicin delivered as heated intraperitoneal chemotherapy (HIPEC) is the preferred intraperitoneal regimen during cytoreductive surgery (CRS). This is one of two dosing schemes listed for this combination. The dose is based on perfusate concentration (50 mg/L cisplatin + 15 mg/L doxorubicin) delivered for 90 minutes. CRS + HIPEC is recommended for medically operable patients with epithelioid histology and complete cytoreduction achievable, without high-risk features. The goal of CRS is macroscopic complete resection (CC-0) or near-complete cytoreduction (CC-1, residual <2.5 mm), which is acceptable for epithelioid histology. Platinum agents (cisplatin and carboplatin) have been associated with improved outcomes over mitomycin C in retrospective comparisons [refs 9,10]. Multi-institutional data show median OS of 53 months, 3-year survival 60%, 5-year survival 47% with CRS + HIPEC [ref 76].

trial_data.description: The Chicago Consensus on peritoneal surface malignancies. Cancer 2020;126:2547-2552.

tier_1_regimens.notes: This is an alternative dosing scheme for cisplatin plus doxorubicin HIPEC, using BSA-based dosing (50 mg/m2 cisplatin + 15 mg/m2 doxorubicin) rather than perfusate concentration-based dosing. The treatment duration is 90 minutes. This regimen is preferred during CRS + HIPEC for eligible patients with peritoneal mesothelioma. See full clinical context in the cisplatin/doxorubicin (perfusion-based) entry above.

trial_data.trial_name: Kusamura et al. 2007; Baratti et al. 2010
trial_data.description: Impact of cytoreductive surgery and HIPEC on systemic toxicity [ref 16/82]. Early and long-term postoperative management following CRS and HIPEC [ref 17/83].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Single-agent cisplatin delivered as HIPEC is a preferred intraperitoneal regimen during CRS. The dose range is 100-240 mg/m2 delivered over 90-110 minutes. Platinum agents have been associated with improved outcomes compared with mitomycin C in retrospective comparisons [refs 9,10]. The NCCN Panel voted cisplatin monotherapy as preferred for IP chemotherapy during CRS + HIPEC [ref 74]. See Principles of Surgery (PEM C) for additional IP chemotherapy regimens and details about completeness of cytoreduction scoring and peritoneal cancer index.

trial_data.description: Comparison of cytoreductive surgery and HIPEC with mitomycin or carboplatin for diffuse malignant peritoneal mesothelioma. Am Surg 2014;80:348-352.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Single-agent carboplatin delivered as HIPEC is a preferred intraperitoneal regimen during CRS. The dose range is 600-800 mg/m2 delivered over 90 minutes. Comparison data suggest carboplatin-based HIPEC has favorable outcomes compared with mitomycin-based HIPEC for peritoneal mesothelioma [ref 83]. The NCCN Panel voted carboplatin as preferred for IP chemotherapy during CRS + HIPEC [ref 74].

trial_data.trial_name: Baratti et al. 2013; Kepenekian et al. 2016
trial_data.description: Diffuse malignant peritoneal mesothelioma: long-term survival with complete CRS followed by HIPEC [ref 19]. Evaluation of systemic chemotherapy with comprehensive treatment through the RENAPE Database [ref 20].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Cisplatin plus mitomycin delivered as HIPEC is a preferred intraperitoneal regimen during CRS. The doses are based on perfusate volume concentration (25 mg/m2/L cisplatin + 3.3 mg/m2/L mitomycin) delivered for 60-90 minutes. The NCCN Panel voted this combination as preferred for IP chemotherapy during CRS + HIPEC [ref 74]. Platinum agents have been associated with improved outcomes compared with mitomycin alone in retrospective comparisons [refs 9,10].

trial_data.description: The Chicago Consensus on peritoneal surface malignancies. Cancer 2020;126:2547-2552.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Mitomycin monotherapy delivered as HIPEC is classified as Useful in Certain Circumstances for peritoneal mesothelioma. The dose is 30 mg/m2 delivered over 90-110 minutes. Platinum-based HIPEC regimens (cisplatin, carboplatin) are preferred over mitomycin C based on retrospective data showing improved outcomes [refs 9,10,82]. Mitomycin may be used in specific clinical circumstances where platinum agents are contraindicated.

drugs.name: Mitomycin
drugs.dose: 30 mg at time 0, followed by 10 mg beginning at 60 minutes

trial_data.description: The Chicago Consensus on peritoneal surface malignancies. Cancer 2020;126:2547-2552.

tier_1_regimens.notes: This is an alternative split-dose mitomycin regimen for HIPEC during CRS. The initial dose of 30 mg is administered at time 0, followed by an additional 10 mg beginning at 60 minutes, with the total treatment duration of 90-110 minutes. This regimen is classified as Useful in Certain Circumstances, as platinum-based regimens are preferred [refs 9,10].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: ALK inhibitor therapy (Ceritinib/Crizotinib) for ALK-rearranged peritoneal mesothelioma

biomarkers.requirement: ALK rearrangement must be identified by IHC or FISH; found in rare patients with peritoneal mesothelioma

trial_data.trial_name: Case reports (Ruschoff et al. 2019; Sakata et al. 2021; Gerthofer et al. 2021; Mian et al. 2020)
trial_data.description: ALK rearrangements identified in rare patients with peritoneal mesothelioma. In 25 young patients (<=40 years) with peritoneal mesothelioma, 2 (8%) had ALK rearrangement [ref 102]. A dramatic response with ceritinib was reported in a 13-year-old girl with peritoneal mesothelioma with ALK rearrangement [ref 104]. STRN-ALK rearranged peritoneal mesothelioma showed dramatic response following ceritinib treatment [ref 28/104]. Case reports have also reported responses to crizotinib [ref 105].

tier_1_regimens.notes: ALK rearrangements have been identified in rare patients with peritoneal mesothelioma, particularly in young patients [refs 49-52,54]. In a study of 25 young patients (<=40 years) with peritoneal mesothelioma, 2 (8%) harbored ALK rearrangements [ref 102]. These patients did not have a history of asbestos exposure or radiation therapy and did not have predisposing germline mutations. Dramatic responses to ALK inhibitors including ceritinib and crizotinib have been reported [refs 28,53,54,104,105]. The NCCN Panel recommends broad molecular tumor profiling with the goal of identifying rare driver alterations including ALK for which effective drugs may be available [refs 27,28]. This is not a standard first-line or subsequent regimen but rather a molecularly targeted approach for the rare subset of patients with ALK-rearranged peritoneal mesothelioma.

tier_2_handbook.definition: Peritoneal mesothelioma is a rare malignancy originating from the mesothelial cells that line the peritoneal cavity, the serosal membrane surrounding the abdominal organs. It accounts for approximately 15% of all mesotheliomas, with the remaining ~85% occurring in the pleura [1]. Mesothelioma is now universally defined as malignant; the term 'malignant' is no longer used in classification [39]. The disease typically presents as a diffuse process involving the peritoneal surfaces, though extremely rare localized variants exist [15-18]. The anatomical boundaries are the entire peritoneal cavity, which includes the parietal and visceral peritoneum, omentum, and mesenteries. Diagnosis often requires distinguishing it from benign conditions like peritoneal inclusion cyst and well-differentiated papillary mesothelial tumor (WDPMT), as well as from metastatic carcinomas [1,44-46]. The World Health Organization (WHO) classification of thoracic tumors (2021) has updated terminology and introduced new entities such as mesothelioma in situ [1,4]. Patients frequently present with abdominal symptoms including ascites (77%), pain (69%), distension, and an abdominal mass (30%), leading to a high symptom burden and often advanced disease at diagnosis [11,21,38]. Management requires a multidisciplinary approach due to the disease's complexity and rarity.

subtypes.name: Epithelioid mesothelioma
subtypes.description: Characterized by epithelioid-to-round cells arranged in diverse architectural patterns such as tubulopapillary, trabecular, solid, acinar, micropapillary, or adenomatoid. This is the most common histologic subtype and is associated with better prognosis compared to other types. Rare variants include clear cell, signet ring cell, rhabdoid, deciduoid, and small cell patterns [5-8].
subtypes.frequency: Most common subtype of peritoneal mesothelioma.
subtypes.key_associations: Better prognosis; median overall survival of 39 months in one study [15]. Often associated with BAP1 loss by immunohistochemistry in ~50%-70% of cases [31-37]. May have lower rates of CDKN2A deletions compared to pleural mesothelioma [40,66,67,73].

subtypes.name: Biphasic (mixed) mesothelioma
subtypes.description: Contains both epithelioid and sarcomatoid components, with each comprising at least 10% of the tumor. The morphologic heterogeneity can make accurate typing challenging, and the proportion of each component may vary across different areas of the tumor [5,14].
subtypes.frequency: Less common than epithelioid but more common than pure sarcomatoid.
subtypes.key_associations: Intermediate prognosis; median overall survival of 14 months [15]. Often considered a high-risk feature for treatment decisions, with surgery sometimes contraindicated unless disease is of low volume [78].

subtypes.name: Sarcomatoid mesothelioma
subtypes.description: Characterized by spindled cells with tapered nuclei. Subtypes include conventional/spindle cell, desmoplastic [9,10], and lymphohistiocytoid [11-13]. A subset may exhibit heterologous differentiation with osteosarcomatous, chondrosarcomatous, and/or rhabdomyosarcomatous elements [10].
subtypes.frequency: Least common of the three main histologic types.
subtypes.key_associations: Poor prognosis. Often shows focal to absent expression for most mesothelial markers on immunohistochemistry, with D2-40 being the most sensitive [27]. BAP1 loss is less frequent (<20%) compared to epithelioid type [38]. Typically considered a high-risk feature precluding upfront surgery in many cases.

subtypes.name: Well-differentiated papillary mesothelial tumor (WDPMT)
subtypes.description: A borderline or potentially low-grade mesothelial neoplasm, often an incidental finding in the peritoneum of individuals assigned female at birth. It is characterized by back-to-back papillae lined by bland mesothelial cells, and may show foci of invasion in some cases, morphologically mimicking mesothelioma [75-77].
subtypes.key_associations: Genetically defined by mutually exclusive mutations in TRAF7 or CDC42 [76]. Distinction from mesothelioma is crucial as treatment options differ; observation may be appropriate for asymptomatic, non-invasive disease.

subtypes.name: Mesothelioma in situ
subtypes.description: A preinvasive, single-layer surface proliferation of neoplastic mesothelial cells without a mass lesion. Diagnosis requires ancillary studies: loss of BAP1 nuclear expression by immunohistochemistry and/or CDKN2A homozygous deletion identified by FISH or MTAP IHC (cytoplasmic staining) [1,22].
subtypes.key_associations: Considered a precursor to invasive mesothelioma. Requires correlation with imaging and clinical findings to confirm absence of a mass lesion.

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: Peritoneal mesothelioma is characterized by recurrent mutations in tumor suppressor genes and epigenetic regulators, with a distinct molecular landscape compared to pleural mesothelioma [70]. The most frequently altered gene is BAP1 (BRCA1-associated protein 1), a tumor suppressor involved in multiple pathways including cell cycle regulation and RNA processing. Inactivation mechanisms include point mutations, copy number loss, structural rearrangements, and minute chromosomal deletions [62-64,67-69]. Aberrant BAP1 protein expression (loss of nuclear staining) is found in ~50%-70% of epithelioid peritoneal mesotheliomas but in <20% of sarcomatoid types [31-37,38]. Other commonly mutated genes include NF2, TP53, SETD2, and others [62-66]. Chromosomal abnormalities are common, with loss of the 9p region containing CDKN2A and 22q containing NF2. Homozygous deletion of CDKN2A by FISH is present in ~60% of mesotheliomas overall, but the prevalence in peritoneal mesotheliomas is lower (8%-26%) [40-44,57-59]. Similarly, loss of MTAP (methylthioadenosine phosphorylase) by IHC, a surrogate for 9p loss, occurs in 14%-16% of peritoneal mesotheliomas [40-44]. Hemizygous loss of NF2 is found in ~50% of pleural mesotheliomas and is also relevant to peritoneal disease [61]. A rare subset of peritoneal mesotheliomas, particularly in younger patients, harbors oncogenic ALK rearrangements (e.g., STRN-ALK), which may confer sensitivity to ALK inhibitor therapy [49-51,53,54]. Germline mutations in cancer susceptibility genes, primarily BAP1 but also BRCA2, CDKN2A, VHL, and others, are present in 12%-16% of patients with pleural and peritoneal mesothelioma, predisposing to earlier onset and family history [48,72,73]. Additionally, rare genomic near-haploidization with extensive loss of heterozygosity has been described, sometimes associated with TP53 and/or SETDB1 mutations [62].

epidemiology.incidence: Peritoneal mesothelioma is a rare cancer, accounting for approximately 15% of all mesotheliomas [1]. In the United States, diffuse mesothelioma as a whole affects ~3,000 patients annually, with an incidence of ~1 in 100,000 [2,3]. Peritoneal mesothelioma specifically is estimated to occur in approximately 300 to 400 people in the United States every year [10]. The true incidence may be higher due to potential misdiagnosis as other peritoneal cancers, such as ovarian cancer.
epidemiology.mortality: The prognosis is generally poor, with a 1-year overall survival rate of approximately 46% and a 5-year overall survival rate of about 20%; cure is rare [11]. Survival is significantly improved for patients who are able to undergo complete cytoreductive surgery (CRS) with intraperitoneal chemotherapy. For example, in a multi-institutional study of 401 patients who underwent CRS and HIPEC, the median overall survival was 53 months, with 5-year survival of 47% [76].
epidemiology.trends: The incidence of pleural mesothelioma and peritoneal mesothelioma has been decreasing in the United States since the 1970s, due to decreased asbestos use [1,5,25-28]. However, asbestos is still imported into the country [28].
epidemiology.demographics: The mean age at diagnosis for peritoneal mesothelioma is about 69 years [11]. Unlike pleural mesothelioma, which is more common in males, peritoneal mesothelioma is diagnosed in roughly equal numbers of males and females [11]. The disease may occur in younger patients, and a subset of cases, particularly those with ALK rearrangements, are seen in children and young adults [102]. Many patients have idiopathic disease, and the link to asbestos exposure is less strong than for pleural mesothelioma [20,21].

risk_factors.detail: Asbestos is the established primary risk factor for mesothelioma in general, but its association with peritoneal mesothelioma is less frequent than with pleural mesothelioma [21-24]. The risk increases with intensity and duration of exposure, and there is a long latency period between exposure and disease development. Although asbestos use has decreased since the 1970s, it is still imported and used in some applications.
risk_factors.strength: Strong for mesothelioma overall; moderate for peritoneal mesothelioma specifically.

risk_factors.detail: Heterozygous germline pathogenic variants in the BAP1 gene cause a hereditary cancer predisposition syndrome, which increases the risk of developing mesothelioma (including peritoneal), uveal melanoma, cholangiocarcinoma, and clear cell renal cell carcinoma [30]. Germline BAP1 mutations are found in 12%-16% of mesothelioma patients and are associated with younger age, family history of cancer, and other synchronous malignancies [48,72,73].

A history of prior radiation therapy to the abdomen has been implicated in some cases of peritoneal mesothelioma, as noted in a case series of young patients with ALK-rearranged tumors who did not have asbestos exposure or germline mutations [102].

risk_factors.factor: Family history of mesothelioma or other cancers
risk_factors.detail: A family history of mesothelioma or other cancers (e.g., uveal melanoma, cholangiocarcinoma) may indicate an underlying germline BAP1 mutation or other genetic predisposition [48,74].

protective_factors: Complete cytoreductive surgery (CRS) with hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy (HIPEC) is associated with significantly improved survival in eligible patients, with median overall survival exceeding 5 years in some series [76,77].
Epithelioid histology is a favorable prognostic factor associated with longer survival compared to biphasic or sarcomatoid histologies [15].
Low disease burden as measured by Peritoneal Cancer Index (PCI) score and the ability to achieve a complete cytoreduction (CC-0 or CC-1) are favorable factors for outcome [2,21].

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Peritoneal mesothelioma (PeM) is a rare cancer originating in mesothelial surfaces of the peritoneum, accounting for approximately 15% of all mesotheliomas, with the remaining approximately 85% arising from the pleura [1]. It is estimated that PeM occurs in approximately 300 to 400 people in the United States every year [10]. The mean age at diagnosis is approximately 69 years [11]. Unlike pleural mesothelioma, which is more common in males, PeM is diagnosed in approximately equal numbers of males and females [11]. PeM may occur in younger patients compared with pleural mesothelioma, which typically occurs in older patients [11]. Patients with PeM commonly present with abdominal signs and symptoms, including ascites (occurring in 77% of patients), abdominal pain (69%), abdominal distension, and an abdominal mass (30%) [11,21,38]. Patients often have a high symptom burden compared with patients who have other types of cancer [11]. Additional presenting symptoms include weight loss, fatigue, anorexia, asthenia, nausea, early satiety, and intestinal obstruction [11,21,38]. Although PeM can spread extensively within the abdomen, it less commonly metastasizes beyond the abdominal cavity [24]. One-year overall survival is approximately 46% in patients with PeM, and 5-year overall survival is about 20%; cure is rare [2,11,121-124]. Survival is improved for patients who are able to undergo complete cytoreductive surgery (CRS) with intraperitoneal chemotherapy [12-16]. Many patients with PeM have idiopathic disease [20]. Similar to pleural mesothelioma, the histologic subtypes of PeM include epithelioid (most common), sarcomatoid, and biphasic (also known as mixed, containing both epithelioid and sarcomatoid components) [4,17-19]. Patients with epithelioid histology have better outcomes than those with either biphasic or sarcomatoid histologies [15].

symptoms.name: Ascites
symptoms.detail: Accumulation of fluid within the peritoneal cavity, present in approximately 77% of patients at diagnosis. May cause abdominal distension, early satiety, and discomfort.

symptoms.name: Abdominal pain
symptoms.detail: Present in approximately 69% of patients. May be diffuse or localized, and may be related to peritoneal tumor burden, adhesions, or organ involvement.

symptoms.name: Abdominal distension
symptoms.detail: Visible or palpable enlargement of the abdomen, which may result from ascites, tumor mass effect, or both.

symptoms.name: Abdominal mass
symptoms.detail: Palpable mass noted on physical examination, present in approximately 30% of patients.

symptoms.name: Weight loss
symptoms.detail: Unintentional weight loss, which may be associated with anorexia, cachexia, or high symptom burden.

symptoms.name: Fatigue
symptoms.detail: Persistent tiredness and lack of energy, common in mesothelioma and often associated with high symptom burden.

symptoms.name: Anorexia
symptoms.detail: Loss of appetite, which may contribute to weight loss and nutritional compromise.

symptoms.name: Asthenia
symptoms.detail: Physical weakness and lack of strength.

symptoms.name: Nausea
symptoms.detail: Feeling of sickness that may lead to vomiting; may result from peritoneal irritation, bowel involvement, or ascites.

symptoms.name: Early satiety
symptoms.detail: Feeling of fullness after eating small amounts of food, often related to ascites or peritoneal disease compressing the stomach.

symptoms.name: Intestinal obstruction
symptoms.detail: Blockage of the bowel that may result from peritoneal tumor encasing or compressing bowel loops. May present with nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and inability to pass flatus or stool.

signs.name: Abdominal distension
signs.detail: Visible enlargement of the abdomen on inspection, commonly due to ascites or bulky peritoneal disease.

signs.name: Palpable abdominal mass
signs.detail: Mass felt on physical examination in approximately 30% of patients.

signs.name: Ascites (shifting dullness, fluid wave)
signs.detail: Clinical signs of peritoneal fluid accumulation on abdominal examination.

signs.name: Peritoneal thickening/nodularity
signs.detail: May be identified on imaging (CT) as diffuse distribution in the abdomen; laparoscopy may reveal peritoneal nodules, masses, or visceral thickening.
signs.frequency: Common (observed on imaging and at surgery)

signs.name: Bulky disease/visceral thickening
signs.detail: Advanced peritoneal disease visible on imaging or at laparoscopy, which may influence surgical candidacy and completeness of cytoreduction.

atypical_or_missed: PeM mimics other diseases and conditions, which may delay diagnosis because the disease is so rare [11,24,38].
Symptoms are nonspecific and overlap with common abdominal conditions, contributing to diagnostic delay [11,21,24,38].
PeM may be misdiagnosed as other cancers that typically involve the peritoneum, such as ovarian cancer [10].
The differential diagnosis of PeM includes peritoneal carcinomatosis, serous peritoneal carcinoma, tuberculous peritonitis, and alcoholic cirrhosis [24,56].
PeM may be confused with benign and preinvasive mesothelial tumors such as peritoneal inclusion cyst, well-differentiated papillary mesothelial tumor (WDPMT), or mesothelioma in situ [44-46].
Many patients have advanced disease at the time of diagnosis due to the delayed recognition of this rare malignancy [11].
Although PeM can spread extensively within the abdomen, it rarely metastasizes beyond the abdominal cavity, which may lead clinicians to underestimate its malignant potential [24].

red_flags: Ascites (present in 77% of patients) requiring urgent investigation to distinguish from benign causes such as liver cirrhosis or heart failure [11,38].
New abdominal mass (present in 30% of patients) that warrants tissue biopsy and multidisciplinary evaluation [11,38].
Unexplained abdominal pain with distension, especially when associated with weight loss, anorexia, or early satiety [11,21,38].
Intestinal obstruction in the setting of peritoneal disease, which may indicate high tumor burden requiring urgent assessment.
Bicavitary disease (involving both the peritoneal and pleural cavities), which is considered a high-risk feature and influences treatment decisions [PEM-2, footnote e].
Performance status (PS) of 3–4, for which best supportive care is recommended rather than active antitumor treatment [PEM-2, footnote d; PEM-B].

Biphasic or sarcomatoid histology, which is associated with significantly worse prognosis compared with epithelioid histology (median survival 13–14 months for biphasic vs. 39–55 months for epithelioid) [15,54] and requires systemic therapy as the primary treatment approach.
High disease burden at diagnosis as indicated by Peritoneal Cancer Index (PCI) greater than 17, which is classified as a high-risk feature and may preclude complete cytoreduction [PEM-2, footnote e].
Thrombocytosis, which is classified as a high-risk feature in the pretreatment evaluation algorithm [PEM-2, footnote e].
Ki-67 index greater than 9%, which is independently prognostic for worse overall survival in patients undergoing CRS + HIPEC and is classified as a high-risk feature [PEM-2, footnote e; 45].

diagnostic.test: CT of chest with contrast + CT or MRI of abdomen/pelvis with contrast
diagnostic.rationale: Initial evaluation imaging to identify peritoneal disease, assess distribution, and evaluate for bicavitary disease. CT with contrast of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis is recommended for initial evaluation [PEM-1]. On CT, diffuse distribution in the abdomen and the absence of lymph nodes or distant metastases suggest PeM; however, there are no specific imaging findings for PeM [24,41,42].
diagnostic.key_findings: Diffuse peritoneal thickening, nodularity, ascites, omental caking, peritoneal implants, visceral involvement. Absence of lymph nodes or distant metastases suggests PeM over metastatic carcinoma. PET/CT helps determine unicavitary vs. bicavitary disease.

diagnostic.rationale: Considered in the initial evaluation to help stage disease and determine extent of peritoneal involvement. PET/CT is used after diagnosis to determine whether patients have unicavitary or bicavitary disease, which influences surgical candidacy [PEM-1].
diagnostic.key_findings: FDG-avid peritoneal deposits; helps distinguish unicavitary from bicavitary disease; may identify occult metastatic disease outside the abdomen.

diagnostic.rationale: Recommended for diagnosis and to assess candidacy for complete cytoreduction. Tissue biopsy of the abdominal mass/nodule(s) with histopathology is essential for an accurate diagnosis of PeM, because symptoms, imaging findings, and serum markers are not specific [PEM-1]. Laparoscopy is also done to assess whether complete CRS is possible [24]. Peritoneal biopsy for diagnosis should provide enough tissue for differentiation between peritoneal inclusion cyst, WDPMT, and subtypes of diffuse mesothelioma such as epithelioid, biphasic, and sarcomatoid [PEM-C].
diagnostic.key_findings: Peritoneal nodules, masses, or thickening; tissue samples for histopathologic evaluation and immunohistochemistry; assessment of peritoneal disease distribution and operability.

diagnostic.rationale: Alternative to laparoscopic biopsy when peritoneal organ masses are present and accessible for percutaneous sampling [PEM-1].
diagnostic.key_findings: Tissue core for histopathologic diagnosis, immunohistochemistry, and molecular testing.

diagnostic.rationale: Optional at diagnosis but useful for prognostication and treatment planning. Ki-67 labeling index by IHC staining is independently prognostic for overall survival for patients undergoing CRS + HIPEC, with Ki-67 index >9% associated with worse survival and therefore considered a high-risk feature [PEM-2, footnote e; 45]. Although measuring the Ki-67 index is not routinely recommended at diagnosis, it may be useful for helping to define high-risk features.
diagnostic.key_findings: Ki-67 >9% is classified as a high-risk feature and is associated with worse survival in patients undergoing CRS + HIPEC.

staging.rationale: There is no accepted TNM staging system for PeM. The PCI scoring system is used to indicate the severity of symptom burden and disease extent within the peritoneal cavity [PEM-C; 21,74]. PCI >17 is classified as a high-risk feature [PEM-2, footnote e].
staging.indication: All patients being evaluated for CRS + HIPEC; used to assess resectability and predict outcomes.

staging.rationale: Classifies the extent of residual disease after cytoreductive surgery. CC-0 (no residual tumor) is the surgical goal; CC-1 (residual tumor <2.5 mm) is also acceptable for epithelioid mesothelioma [PEM-C; 21]. CC score >1 is classified as a high-risk feature [PEM-2, footnote e].
staging.indication: Assessed at the time of cytoreductive surgery; CC score informs postoperative treatment decisions and prognosis.

staging.test: CT chest + CT or MRI of abdomen/pelvis with contrast (staging/surveillance)
staging.rationale: Recommended for staging and post-treatment surveillance. Surveillance schedule: every 3–6 months for 5 years, then yearly [PEM-2, footnote h].
staging.indication: All patients after primary treatment for ongoing disease assessment.

biomarkers.rationale: Serum biomarker that may correlate with disease status. Measurement may be considered in patients suspected of having PeM [PEM-1; 21,42,43]. Levels may correlate with disease status but are not diagnostic alone.
biomarkers.when: Optional at initial evaluation for patients suspected of having PeM.

biomarkers.rationale: Serum marker that may correlate with disease status in PeM. Measurement may be considered alongside SMRP [21,42].

BAP1 is a tumor suppressor implicated in the pathogenesis of mesothelioma. Aberrant BAP1 protein expression (defined as absence of nuclear BAP1 IHC staining or cytoplasmic staining) is present in approximately 50%–70% of epithelioid mesothelioma but in less than 20% of sarcomatoid mesothelioma [PEM-A; 31-38]. BAP1 IHC is a specific (though not sensitive) marker to distinguish mesothelioma from reactive mesothelial proliferations. It is also useful for diagnosing mesothelioma in situ [22,66-71].
biomarkers.when: At pathologic diagnosis, to distinguish mesothelioma from reactive mesothelial proliferations and benign mesothelial tumors.

biomarkers.rationale: Methylthioadenosine phosphorylase IHC loss is used as a surrogate for CDKN2A deletions. Loss of cytoplasmic MTAP staining has been reported in approximately 40%–60% of mesothelioma but rarely in reactive proliferations [PEM-A; 35-37]. However, CDKN2A FISH and MTAP IHC are less useful for peritoneal mesotheliomas because the prevalence of CDKN2A deletions is 8%–26% and MTAP loss is 14%–16% in peritoneal mesotheliomas [40-44].
biomarkers.when: At pathologic diagnosis, when distinguishing mesothelioma from reactive mesothelial proliferations.

biomarkers.rationale: Homozygous loss of CDKN2A by FISH testing is present in approximately 60% of mesotheliomas overall [57-59]. However, CDKN2A deletions are found in only 8%–35% of peritoneal mesotheliomas compared with 60%–74% of pleural mesotheliomas [40,66,67,73]. CDKN2A loss can aid in the distinction of mesothelioma from reactive mesothelial proliferations, but CDKN2A loss alone is not useful in separating mesothelioma from other tumor types [60].
biomarkers.when: At pathologic diagnosis, as an ancillary test to confirm malignancy.

biomarkers.test: Immunohistochemistry panel (calretinin, D2-40/podoplanin, claudin-4, TTF-1, polyclonal CEA, PAX8)
biomarkers.rationale: No single IHC marker is entirely sensitive and specific. A panel including at least two mesothelial markers (e.g., calretinin, D2-40) and two carcinoma markers (e.g., claudin-4, TTF-1, polyclonal CEA, PAX8) should be used to establish the diagnosis [PEM-A; 26]. For peritoneal mesothelioma, IHC markers differ slightly from those for pleural mesothelioma; for example, TTF-1 and D2-40 are noted as not useful for diagnosing PeM specifically, although they are useful for pleural mesothelioma [17]. Calretinin is a positive mesothelial marker; claudin-4, TTF-1, and polyclonal CEA are negative (carcinoma) markers [23-25]. PAX8 is a carcinoma marker, but sometimes PeMs will stain for PAX8 [17,65].
biomarkers.when: At pathologic diagnosis for all cases.

biomarkers.rationale: Recommended with the goal of identifying rare driver alterations (e.g., NTRK or ALK) for which effective drugs may be available or to appropriately counsel patients regarding the availability of clinical trials [PEM-D, footnote b; 27,28]. Somatic ALK rearrangements have been identified in rare patients with peritoneal mesothelioma (approximately 8% in one series of young patients) who have shown dramatic response with ALK inhibitor therapies [PEM-A; 49-52,53,54].
biomarkers.when: At diagnosis or upon disease progression, to identify actionable mutations.

biomarkers.rationale: Explored as a potential diagnostic marker for sarcomatoid mesotheliomas, since GATA3 is expressed in only approximately 10%–20% of sarcomatoid carcinoma but strongly expressed in all sarcomatoid/desmoplastic mesotheliomas [PEM-A; 28,29].
biomarkers.when: Considered in the differential diagnosis of sarcomatoid mesothelioma vs. sarcomatoid carcinoma.

do_not_miss: Histopathologic tissue biopsy (not fine-needle aspiration or cytology alone) is essential for accurate diagnosis and histologic subtype determination, which directly directs treatment [PEM-1; 21,24].
BAP1 IHC for distinguishing mesothelioma from reactive mesothelial proliferations and for diagnosing mesothelioma in situ [PEM-A; 22].
IHC panel with at least two mesothelial markers and two carcinoma markers for definitive diagnosis [PEM-A; 26].
Ki-67 index, which may be useful for identifying high-risk features (Ki-67 >9%) and guiding adjuvant therapy decisions [PEM-2; 45].
Assessment of histologic subtype (epithelioid, biphasic, or sarcomatoid), as this is critical for treatment planning and prognosis [PEM-A; 1,15].
CT of chest with contrast + CT or MRI of abdomen/pelvis with contrast for initial staging [PEM-1].
Assessment for bicavitary disease to determine surgical candidacy [PEM-2].
Broad molecular tumor profiling to identify ALK rearrangements or other rare driver alterations amenable to targeted therapy [PEM-D; 27,28].
Multidisciplinary team evaluation with experience in peritoneal mesothelioma recommended for all patients [PEM-1].
avoid: Fine-needle aspiration (FNA) of the nodule/mass is NOT recommended for diagnosis, because FNA cannot differentiate between the different histologic subtypes of PeM including epithelioid, sarcomatoid, and biphasic [Discussion; 21,24].
Paracentesis fluid (cytology) alone is NOT recommended for diagnosis, because invasion cannot be detected using cytology [Discussion; 21,24].
Cytology alone is generally not considered adequate for the important histologic differentiation required for treatment decisions [PEM-C].
Relying on any single IHC marker for diagnosis, as no individual marker is entirely sensitive and specific [PEM-A; 26].

Interpreting 'worrisome' features (necrosis, cytologic atypia, mitoses) as definitive evidence of malignancy in isolation, since each can be present in reactive pleuritis and does not necessarily indicate malignancy [PEM-A; 19,20].

staging.system: There is no accepted TNM staging system for peritoneal mesothelioma. The Peritoneal Cancer Index (PCI) scoring system is used to indicate disease burden/severity of symptom burden, and the Completeness of Cytoreduction (CC) score is used to classify residual disease after surgery [PEM-C; 21,74]. A novel TNM staging system based on the peritoneal carcinomatosis index has been described but is not universally adopted [81].

stage_groups.criteria: The PCI scoring system divides the abdomen into 13 regions (9 abdominal regions and 4 small bowel regions). Each region is scored from 0 to 3 based on the size of tumor implants: 0 = no tumor; 1 = implants up to 0.5 cm; 2 = implants 0.5 cm to 5 cm; 3 = implants >5 cm or confluence. Total PCI score ranges from 0 to 39 [PEM-C; 21]. PCI >17 is classified as a high-risk feature associated with lower likelihood of complete cytoreduction [PEM-2, footnote e].
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: PCI quantifies the extent of peritoneal disease and helps predict the likelihood of achieving complete cytoreduction. Higher PCI scores indicate greater disease burden and are associated with worse outcomes.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Not specified numerically in the source document for specific PCI ranges. However, incomplete cytoreduction (CC-2 or CC-3) is associated with significantly worse survival than complete resection (CC-0).
stage_groups.treatment_intent: PCI score guides surgical decision-making. Patients with very high PCI scores may not be candidates for complete cytoreduction, and systemic therapy may be preferred.

stage_groups.criteria: CC-0: No residual tumor; CC-1: Residual tumor <2.5 mm; CC-2: Residual tumor 2.5–25 mm; CC-3: Residual tumor >25 mm [PEM-C; 21].
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: CC-0 (macroscopic complete resection) is the surgical goal. CC-1 is also acceptable for epithelioid mesothelioma, as a large multi-institutional study suggests less than 2% change in 5-year overall survival and unchanged median OS for epithelioid peritoneal mesothelioma undergoing CC-1 compared with CC-0 [PEM-C; 2]. CC score >1 (CC-2 or CC-3) is classified as a high-risk feature [PEM-2, footnote e].
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: For biphasic histology: CC-0 resection associated with 5-year survival of 50.2% (median 6.8 years); CC-1 resection associated with 5-year survival of 41.6% (median 2.8 years); CC-2 resection associated with median survival of only 4.3 months [78]. For epithelioid histology: meta-analysis of 1047 patients showed 5-year survival of 42% [16]. Multi-institutional study of 401 patients showed 3-year survival of 60% and 5-year survival of 47% [76].
stage_groups.treatment_intent: CC-0 or CC-1 achieves the goal of cytoreductive surgery. CC-2 or CC-3 indicates incomplete cytoreduction; surgery should generally be aborted if macroscopic complete resection is not achievable, unless palliative benefit with minimal morbidity is anticipated [PEM-C; 3].

stage_groups.criteria: Low-risk features: epithelioid histology AND absence of ANY high-risk features. High-risk features (any one): biphasic/sarcomatoid histology, nodal metastasis, Ki-67 >9%, thrombocytosis, PS = 2, bicavitary disease, high disease burden/incomplete cytoreduction (PCI >17, CC score >1) [PEM-2, footnotes c,e].
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Risk stratification determines the treatment pathway. Patients who are medically operable with complete cytoreduction achievable AND low-risk features proceed directly to CRS + HIPEC. Patients with ANY high-risk feature or who are medically inoperable follow an alternative treatment pathway involving systemic therapy and possible neoadjuvant treatment before reevaluation for CRS + HIPEC [PEM-2].
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Not specified by individual risk feature in the source document.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Low-risk: Upfront CRS + HIPEC with surveillance (or adjuvant systemic therapy if high-risk features are identified intraoperatively). High-risk: Systemic therapy first, then consider CRS + HIPEC if medically operable and complete cytoreduction becomes achievable [PEM-2].

staging_pearls: There is NO accepted TNM staging system for peritoneal mesothelioma, unlike many other solid tumors. The Peritoneal Cancer Index (PCI) and Completeness of Cytoreduction (CC) score are the primary tools for assessing disease extent and surgical outcomes [PEM-C; 21,74].
PeM is currently classified by the WHO (2021) as diffuse mesothelioma; the term 'malignant' is no longer used because all mesotheliomas are now defined as malignant [PEM-A; 39].
A novel TNM staging system based on the peritoneal carcinomatosis index has been proposed [81] but is not part of the standard NCCN recommendations.
Biphasic mesothelioma is defined as containing both epithelioid and sarcomatoid components, with each comprising at least 10% of the tumor [PEM-A].

The accuracy of histologic typing increases with a higher number of biopsies; while sarcomatoid histology in biopsies is highly predictive of sarcomatoid histology in resections, epithelioid histology in biopsies is not entirely specific and may be changed to biphasic or sarcomatoid in up to 20% of patients on resection [PEM-A; 14].
For bicavitary disease (involving both peritoneal and pleural cavities), systemic therapy is recommended. Surgery can be considered in select cases with minimal thoracic disease burden [PEM-C; 6].

There are three histologic types with distinct prognostic and treatment implications: epithelioid (best prognosis, median OS 39 months), biphasic (intermediate, median OS 14 months in some series), and sarcomatoid (worst prognosis) [15,54].

PeM and pleural mesothelioma have distinct molecular features. Peritoneal mesothelioma has lower rates of CDKN2A deletions (8%–35%) compared with pleural mesothelioma (60%–74%), and MTAP loss is 14%–16% in peritoneal mesotheliomas [40,40,66,67,73].

Germline mutations (particularly BAP1) are present in approximately 12%–16% of patients with pleural and peritoneal mesothelioma and are more common in patients who are young, have a family history of mesothelioma, or have synchronous malignancies [48,72,73]. Patients with germline BAP1 mutations have prolonged survival [47,48].

Epidemiologic note: PeM occurs in approximately equal numbers of males and females, whereas pleural mesothelioma is more common in males. PeM is less frequently associated with asbestos exposure compared with pleural mesothelioma [11,21-24].
Repeat CRS + HIPEC can be considered in patients who are more than 12 months from prior CRS and otherwise considered to have operable disease [PEM-2, footnote j; 13,14].
All NCCN recommendations in this guideline are Category 2A (based on lower-level evidence, uniform NCCN consensus ≥85% that the intervention is appropriate) unless otherwise indicated [CAT-1].

management_principles.overview: Peritoneal mesothelioma (PeM) is a rare cancer, with an estimated 300-400 new cases annually in the US, representing approximately 15% of all mesotheliomas. The mean age at diagnosis is about 69 years. The 1-year overall survival (OS) is approximately 46%, and the 5-year OS is about 20%, with cure being rare [11, 121-124]. Treatment philosophy centers on a multimodal approach, primarily involving cytoreductive surgery (CRS) with intraperitoneal chemotherapy for eligible patients, or systemic therapy for those who are not surgical candidates. Histologic subtype (epithelioid, biphasic, sarcomatoid) is a critical determinant of prognosis and treatment strategy. Epithelioid histology carries a better prognosis (median OS ~55 months with CRS) compared to biphasic (median OS ~13 months) [15]. All treatment decisions should be made by a multidisciplinary team with experience in peritoneal mesothelioma [12, 21].

by_intent.patient_group: Medically operable patients with epithelioid histology and unicavitary disease who can achieve complete cytoreduction.
by_intent.approach: Primary treatment with cytoreductive surgery (CRS) + hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy (HIPEC). For low-risk features, surveillance follows. For high-risk features, adjuvant systemic therapy is considered [PEM-2, PEM-D].

by_intent.patient_group: Patients who are medically inoperable, cannot achieve complete cytoreduction, have high-risk features (biphasic/sarcomatoid histology, nodal metastasis, Ki-67 >9%, etc.), bicavitary disease, or recurrent disease after prior CRS+HIPEC.
by_intent.approach: Systemic therapy as primary treatment. Surgery may be considered in select cases (e.g., low-volume biphasic disease). Best supportive care is recommended for patients with performance status (PS) 3–4 [PEM-2, PEM-B].

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Management by a multidisciplinary team with experience in peritoneal mesothelioma is explicitly recommended. This team should include surgical oncologists with expertise in CRS+HIPEC, medical oncologists, and diagnostic imaging specialists to evaluate surgical candidacy and treatment options [12, 21].
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Best supportive care is recommended for patients presenting with performance status (PS) 3–4 [PEM-2 footnote d]. For patients with PS 0–2, treatment with systemic therapy or consideration for CRS+HIPEC (if eligible) is appropriate.

nodes.criteria: Patient is medically operable, complete cytoreduction is achievable, and has low-risk features (defined as epithelioid histology and absence of ANY high-risk features).

adjuvant_logic.summary: If no high-risk features are identified post-surgery, imaging surveillance is sufficient.

nodes.criteria: Patient is medically operable and complete cytoreduction is achievable, but presence of ANY surgical/pathologic high-risk feature (biphasic/sarcomatoid histology, nodal metastasis, Ki-67 >9%, thrombocytosis, PS=2, bicavitary disease, high disease burden/PCI >17, CC >1).

adjuvant_logic.summary: If high-risk features are identified post-surgery, adjuvant systemic therapy is recommended.

management_pathways.title: Medically Inoperable or Complete Cytoreduction Not Achievable

nodes.criteria: Patient is medically inoperable, complete cytoreduction is not achievable, OR presence of ANY high-risk feature precluding surgery.
Subsequent evaluation after initial systemic therapy shows patient is now medically operable and complete cytoreduction is achievable.
Patient has progression of disease after prior CRS and HIPEC.

options.label: Repeat CRS + HIPEC (if >12 months from prior procedure and otherwise operable)

management_pathways.title: Systemic Therapy for Biphasic or Sarcomatoid Histology

nodes.criteria: Patient has biphasic or sarcomatoid peritoneal mesothelioma and requires systemic therapy (e.g., medically inoperable, high-risk features).

items.text: Abdominal signs and symptoms: ascites (77%), pain (69%), distension, and an abdominal mass (30%). Other symptoms may include weight loss, fatigue, anorexia, asthenia, nausea, early satiety, and intestinal obstruction.
items.recommendation: High index of suspicion required due to nonspecific symptoms; diagnosis may be delayed.

items.text: CT of chest with contrast + CT or MRI of abdomen/pelvis with contrast.
items.recommendation: Mandatory initial evaluation. Diffuse distribution and absence of lymph nodes or distant metastases suggest PeM, but findings are not specific [PEM-1].
To help determine unicavitary vs. bicavitary disease and assess for metastases outside the abdomen.

items.text: Midline laparoscopy with biopsies of recurrent nodule/mass, image-guided core biopsy, or peritoneal organ metastasis biopsies.
items.recommendation: Essential for diagnosis. FNA is NOT recommended for diagnosis as it cannot differentiate histologic subtypes. Cytology (e.g., from paracentesis) is also not recommended as invasion cannot be detected [PEM-1].
Optional but considered a high-risk feature if >9%, impacting treatment decisions [PEM-2 footnote e].
May be considered; levels may correlate with disease status.

items.text: Histologic subtyping (epithelioid, biphasic, sarcomatoid) and immunohistochemistry (IHC) panel.
items.recommendation: Mandatory. Use a panel including at least two mesothelial markers (e.g., calretinin, D2-40) and two carcinoma markers (e.g., claudin-4, TTF-1, polyclonal CEA, PAX8) [PEM-A]. BAP1 IHC loss is a specific marker for malignancy.

items.text: Assessment for high-risk features: biphasic/sarcomatoid histology, nodal metastasis, Ki-67 >9%, thrombocytosis, PS=2, bicavitary disease, high disease burden (PCI >17), incomplete cytoreduction (CC >1).
items.recommendation: Critical for risk stratification and treatment planning [PEM-2 footnote e].

items.text: Assessment during laparoscopy for candidacy for complete cytoreduction.
items.recommendation: Laparoscopy is recommended to determine if complete cytoreduction is achievable [PEM-C].
Used to quantify disease burden and guide surgical decisions. PCI >17 is a high-risk feature [PEM-C].
PS 3-4 indicates best supportive care only. PS 2 is a high-risk feature [PEM-2].
Refer to the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List [PEM-1].

surgery.role: Surgical resection (CRS + HIPEC) is a primary, potentially curative treatment modality for selected patients with peritoneal mesothelioma. It is recommended for medically operable patients with epithelioid histology and unicavitary disease where complete cytoreduction is achievable.
principles: Surgical resection should be performed by surgical oncologists with experience in managing peritoneal mesothelioma [PEM-C].

The goal of CRS is macroscopic complete resection (CC-0: no residual tumor). Near-complete cytoreduction (CC-1: residual tumor <2.5 mm) is also acceptable for epithelioid mesothelioma, as studies suggest minimal impact on 5-year OS compared to CC-0 [2].

If macroscopic complete resection or near-complete cytoreduction is not possible, surgery should be aborted. Palliative surgery and/or HIPEC can be considered only if associated with minimal morbidity [3, PEM-C].
Complete cytoreduction frequently requires total parietal peritonectomy, including visceral resections when necessary [4].

Decisions are highly dependent on accurate histology. Peritoneal biopsy for diagnosis must provide enough tissue for differentiation between entities like peritoneal inclusion cyst, WDPMT, and diffuse mesothelioma subtypes. Cytology is not adequate [PEM-C].

procedures.name: Cytoreductive Surgery (CRS) + Hyperthermic Intraperitoneal Chemotherapy (HIPEC)
procedures.approach: Typically involves midline laparotomy with peritonectomy procedures and visceral resections to achieve CC-0 or CC-1. Followed by intraoperative administration of heated intraperitoneal chemotherapy.
procedures.indications: Medically operable patients with peritoneal mesothelioma (predominantly epithelioid histology) where complete cytoreduction is achievable. May be considered in select cases of low-volume biphasic disease or bicavitary disease with minimal thoracic burden.
procedures.key_complications: Major morbidity reported in approximately 26-39% of patients. Acute kidney injury occurred in 35.7% in one series. Perioperative mortality reported at 1-2% in high-volume centers [14, 77].
procedures.operative_mortality: Reported as 1-2% (9/401 patients in one large study) [76].

surgery.resectability_criteria: Resectability is defined by the ability to achieve a macroscopic complete resection (CC-0) or near-complete resection (CC-1) with acceptable morbidity. Contraindications to surgery include: medical inoperability, the presence of ANY high-risk feature (biphasic/sarcomatoid histology, nodal metastasis, Ki-67 >9%, thrombocytosis, PS=2, bicavitary disease, high disease burden with PCI >17 or CC score >1) unless surgery is reconsidered after neoadjuvant therapy [PEM-2, PEM-C].
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: Patients should undergo thorough multidisciplinary evaluation. If a bevacizumab-containing regimen is administered preoperatively, there should be at least a 6-week interval between the last dose and CRS to minimize surgical complications [7, 8, PEM-C]. For patients with high-risk features (e.g., biphasic/sarcomatoid histology, high PCI), neoadjuvant systemic therapy is strongly encouraged before re-evaluation for CRS+HIPEC [PEM-2].

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation therapy is not recommended as a primary therapy for peritoneal mesothelioma. Its role is limited and selective, primarily for palliation of specific symptoms.
principles: Primary systemic disease within the peritoneal cavity is not amenable to effective radiation therapy due to the diffuse nature of the disease and risk of toxicity to surrounding organs.
Radiation may be considered for palliation of localized, symptomatic problems such as painful bone metastases or focal tumor deposits causing pain or bleeding.

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy is a cornerstone of treatment for patients who are not candidates for CRS+HIPEC, have high-risk features precluding upfront surgery, or develop recurrence after local therapies. Recommendations are primarily extrapolated from clinical trials in pleural mesothelioma, as PeM is rare and dedicated trials are limited [21, 74]. Regimens are stratified by histology (epithelioid vs. biphasic/sarcomatoid) and line of therapy. Broad molecular tumor profiling is recommended to identify rare driver alterations (e.g., ALK rearrangements) for which targeted therapies may be available [27, 28, PEM-D footnote b].

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: (Carboplatin or Cisplatin)/Pemetrexed + Bevacizumab; (Carboplatin or Cisplatin)/Pemetrexed; Ipilimumab + Nivolumab [1-11].
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on evidence from pleural mesothelioma trials (e.g., MAPS, CheckMate 743) extrapolated to PeM. Combination immunotherapy (Ipi/Nivo) is a preferred option based on improved survival data in pleural disease [11].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: (Carboplatin or Cisplatin)/Gemcitabine; Pemetrexed alone; Vinorelbine (Useful in Certain Circumstances) [12-16].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 testing is not required before initiating immune checkpoint inhibitors based on current data [85, 88]. ALK rearrangement testing is recommended to identify patients who may respond to ALK inhibitors [32, 37, 53, 54].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: First-Line Therapy for Biphasic or Sarcomatoid Histology
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: CheckMate 743 demonstrated superior OS for Ipi/Nivo versus chemotherapy in unresectable pleural mesothelioma, including non-epithelioid histology [11].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: (Carboplatin or Cisplatin)/Pemetrexed + Bevacizumab; (Carboplatin or Cisplatin)/Pemetrexed (Other Recommended) [1-9].

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: If initial therapy was chemotherapy: Nivolumab or Ipilimumab + Nivolumab. If initial therapy was Ipi/Nivo: (Carboplatin or Cisplatin)/Pemetrexed +/- Bevacizumab [17-19].
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: CONFIRM trial showed nivolumab improved OS versus placebo in relapsed mesothelioma after platinum-based chemotherapy [18]. Pemetrexed rechallenge can be considered if good prior response [29, PEM-D footnote e].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Pemetrexed; Atezolizumab + Bevacizumab (only if no prior ICI exposure); Gemcitabine; Vinorelbine (Other Recommended) [20-26].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: As above. Atezolizumab + Bevacizumab should only be considered if patients have not been previously treated with immune checkpoint inhibitors [22, PEM-D footnote h].

key_regimens.name: Pemetrexed + Platinum
key_regimens.setting: First-line and subsequent therapy for epithelioid histology; subsequent therapy for biphasic/sarcomatoid.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia, anemia, thrombocytopenia, nausea, vomiting, fatigue, nephrotoxicity (with cisplatin).

key_regimens.name: Pemetrexed + Platinum + Bevacizumab
key_regimens.key_toxicities: As above plus hypertension, proteinuria, bleeding, thromboembolic events.

key_regimens.name: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab
key_regimens.setting: First-line for biphasic/sarcomatoid; first-line and subsequent for epithelioid.
key_regimens.cycle_days: Every 3 weeks for 4 cycles (induction), then nivolumab 240 mg IV every 2 weeks or 480 mg IV every 4 weeks for maintenance.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-mediated adverse events (colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies, dermatologic toxicity), fatigue.

key_regimens.name: Atezolizumab + Bevacizumab
key_regimens.setting: Subsequent therapy only if no prior ICI exposure.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Hypertension, proteinuria, fatigue, immune-mediated adverse events.

key_regimens.name: Nivolumab Monotherapy
key_regimens.setting: Subsequent therapy if chemotherapy was first-line.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Fatigue, rash, pruritus, immune-mediated adverse events.

treatment_response_assessment.title: Imaging Surveillance and Response Assessment
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Imaging surveillance is recommended after primary treatment (CRS+HIPEC or systemic therapy) and for monitoring during follow-up.
response_logic: After CRS+HIPEC: For low-risk patients (epithelioid, no high-risk features), imaging surveillance is sufficient. For high-risk patients, adjuvant systemic therapy is considered, followed by imaging surveillance.

During systemic therapy: Response is typically assessed using standard imaging criteria (e.g., RECIST) to determine progression or benefit, guiding decisions on continuing, changing, or stopping therapy.
At progression: Triggers consideration of next-line systemic therapy or, in select cases, repeat CRS+HIPEC if >12 months from prior procedure and otherwise operable.
imaging_recommendations: CT chest + CT or MRI of abdomen/pelvis with contrast.
Frequency: Every 3–6 months for the first 5 years, then yearly thereafter [PEM-2 footnote h].
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: Repeat biopsy is not routinely recommended for response assessment but may be considered at progression to re-evaluate histology or obtain tissue for molecular testing (e.g., ALK rearrangement).
Repeat CRS+HIPEC can be considered in patients who are >12 months from prior CRS and are otherwise considered to have operable disease upon re-evaluation [PEM-2 footnote j].
